texto: Reading is sometimes thought of as a form of escapism, and it’s a common turn of phrase to speak of getting lost in a book. But a book can also be where one finds oneself; and when a reader is grasped and held by a book, reading does not feel like an escape from life so much as it feels like an urgent, crucial dimension of life itself.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rebecca Mead: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “Reading is sometimes thought of as a form of escapism, and it’s a common turn of phrase to speak …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, books, literature pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de literature e reading.

frase_original: Reading is sometimes thought of as a form of escapism, and it’s a common turn of phrase to speak of getting lost in a book. But a book can also be where one finds oneself; and when a reader is grasped and held by a book, reading does not feel like an escape from life so much as it feels like an urgent, crucial dimension of life itself.

texto: But to demand that a work be “relatable” expresses a different expectation: that the work itself be somehow accommodating to, or reflective of, the experience of the reader or viewer. The reader or viewer remains passive in the face of the book or movie or play: she expects the work to be done for her. If the concept of identification suggested that an individual experiences a work as a mirror in which he might recognize himself, the notion of relatability implies that the work in question serves like a selfie: a flattering confirmation of an individual’s solipsism.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rebecca Mead: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a trabalho. O trecho “But to demand that a work be “relatable” expresses a different expectation: that the work itself …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que trabalho, art, criticism pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de trabalho e criticism.

If the concept of identification suggested that an individual experiences a work as a mirror in which he might recognize himself, the notion of relatability implies that the work in question serves like a selfie: a flattering confirmation of an individual’s solipsism.

texto: In the 'Life' of George Eliot, John Walter Cross gave an intriguing account of Eliot's creative method. "She told me that, in all her best writing, there was a 'not herself' which took possession of her, and that she felt her own personality to be merely the instrument through which this spirit, as it were, was acting," Cross wrote.
